entries.title: A Beginner’s Guide to Growing Mushrooms at Home (2026)
entries.summary: From countertop kits to a bathroom bucket full of straw and beyond, I spent a year testing popular mushroom-growing methods. Here’s what fruited—and what just grew mold.
entries.content:
Mushroom hunting is cool and fun, but you know what’s not cool and fun? Having slugs or other mushroom hunters discover your secret spot. Or, worst of all, accidentally collecting the wrong kind of mushroom that could make you quite sick—or worse.

Ideally, you’d be able to grow your favorite kind of mushrooms safely in your own home or yard, whether that’s shiitakes, morels, or lion’s manes, just like you would vegetables. But like many gardening-adjacent pursuits, it’s not that simple. Mushrooms, like all fungi, need just three things to grow: spores, a food source, and a humid environment.

However, because mushrooms are incredibly sensitive to light, heat, and contamination, it doesn’t take much to create a prohibitive growing environment. And then there’s the question of whether it’s possible to simulate the critical symbiotic relationships that many mushrooms, especially more coveted ones like porcinis and chanterelles, must maintain with tree roots to thrive.

I am far from a mycology expert, but during the past year, I, a busy full-time-working parent, have engaged in five different attempts to grow mushrooms in my house and backyard: shiitakes on an inoculated log, oysters on a cardboard-enclosed fruiting block, wine caps in spawn buried in bark chips in my garden, oysters from grain spawn in a bucket, and oysters and lion’s manes in a monotub setup.

I laughed, I cried, I made an epic mess of chopped straw in my bathroom, and only two of the five methods succeeded. If you, too, are mushroom-curious and looking to grow your own, here’s what I did—and, in some cases, wish I had done differently.

Also, be sure to check out our guides to mushroom supplements (the functional kind), mushroom gummies, indoor gardening systems, and mushroom gifts.

Which Will Prevail?

Method 1: Mushroom Log

Difficulty 3/10 (Soak periodically and maintain humidity) Aesthetics 9/10 (It’s a log, but you must admit it’s a nice-looking log) Success 0/10 (So far, at least)

There’s nothing more natural, simple, and affordable than a mushroom log. Many mushroom logs available for purchase are meant exclusively for outdoor use, as they’re just plain old logs with sealed holes full of colonized mushroom spawn. But this 12-inch-tall option from Williams Sonoma is meant for indoor display (though you can also place it outside if you’d prefer), with smooth waxed ends and a rustic metal tag printed with its creation date.

Soak it for 24 hours and—theoretically—you’ll have mushrooms pinning in about 30 days. Even though oyster mushrooms are the easiest variety to grow, for reasons known only to me at the time, I chose a shiitake log, which came with a piece of paper identifying it as a “younger log.” This means the spawn was still in the process of colonizing and should fruit seven to nine months from the date stamped on its tag, which was December 2025. I soaked the log in mid-May and again in early July, but I’m still waiting to see any pins. Williams Sonoma says the log is guaranteed to fruit, so it continues to sit on my dining room table, in a saucer full of water, tented with a plastic produce bag. Some green mold has started to appear on the top, but I remain optimistic, even though this is not my first shiitake mushroom log failure (I tried to grow one from a different brand some years ago, outside under a bush).

If you’d rather just use an existing log or logs outdoors on your property, buy spawn plugs for your preferred variety, drill holes for them, and seal the holes with wax. (You can buy the spawn plugs themselves, or in a kit with the wax and proper drill bit.) To learn which type of wood might be the best host for which kinds of mushrooms, North Spore has a helpful tutorial.

Method 2: Spray-and-Grow Box

Difficulty 4/10 (Requires daily spraying) Aesthetics 7/10 (Looks quite nice on a table or windowsill) Success 9/10 (Some mushrooms, but not a ton)

If you’re brand-new to mushroom growing, don’t need large quantities, are happy to skip unusual varieties, and just want near-guaranteed success without fussing over grain spawn and humidistats, an oyster mushroom boxed kit is a good place to start. Oyster mushrooms are hardy; fast-growing; and easy to incorporate into cooking, with a mild flavor not unlike the white button mushrooms you see in the grocery store.

Two brands of boxed kits I've tried and liked are Back to the Roots and North Spore. The former comes with a dehydrated fruiting block. When you’re ready to use it, soak it for six to 10 hours, set it upright in a location with indirect light, and mist it regularly to keep up the humidity. After taking these steps, you should have a bumper crop of mushrooms within days.

North Spore’s blocks ($30) are shipped fresh, so they don’t need to be rehydrated and will produce flushes (the name for each mushroom “crop”) over and over again, but because the fresh block is more moist, it can attract fungus gnats. When I had this problem, North Spore recommended I add a few drops of dish soap, a tablespoon of vinegar, and a tablespoon of baking soda per cup of water, and spray the mixture on the block before the mushrooms fruit. Unfortunately, my fungus-gnat problem cropped up after the mushrooms had fruited, so it was messy and challenging to spray the block underneath them. If I were doing this over again with a fresh block, I would probably use a nonfungicide insecticide, like a dissolved mosquito dunk, and treat the block immediately upon opening.

If you know you don’t like oyster mushrooms, North Spore also offers a lion’s mane spray-and-grow kit for $35. I haven’t tried the fruiting block in a box, but I did try it in the Boomr Bin, below, and had great success.

Method 3: Sawdust Spawn Right in the Garden

Difficulty 6/10 (Crumble and mix in with wood chips in a shaded area) Aesthetics 5/10 (Neutral—it will look like your garden, with wood chips or straw) Success 2/10 (Mushrooms grew, just not the right ones)

If you wish mushrooms just grew naturally in your yard and don’t want to deal with inoculating a log, there is an alternate path: Buy a bag of spawn, crumble it up, and sprinkle it over wood chips or a similar substrate upon which the developing fungus can feed. The wine cap mushroom is especially useful for this purpose, as its mycelium—a weblike fungus network that functions as a mushroom’s roots—will help break down the wood chips or substrate, improving soil texture and keeping weeds at bay naturally. (As a bonus, wine caps are edible.)

You can also do this right in your garden beds using straw, which acts as a natural mulch and food source for the fungus. Or you can do it on wood chips between garden beds. I tried the wood chip method with both wine cap and morel spawn (the latter from a different company), both of which arrived in vacuum-sealed blocks. I broke them up, crumbled them using my hands, and mixed them into the wood chips. However, my yard is lousy with squirrels; I’m in a constant battle to keep them away from my bird feeders, and they seem to have found a new way to aggravate me, digging up all the crumbled spawn and eating it before it could fruit. (I did, however, get a couple of Leratiomyces ceres, or chip cherry mushrooms, which are not edible.)

Method 4: Fruiting Blocks in a Monotub

Difficulty 5/10 (Though this depends on what you have in it) Aesthetics 4/10 (It looks like a science experiment) Success 10/10 (No notes—it works)

The best setup I’ve tried, and the one I would absolutely use again, is a monotub fruiting chamber, which is basically a fancy name for a plastic bin with a lid. If you really want to do it up, you might consider an automated version, which is designed to balance CO2, oxygen, and humidity. You can make your own, but North Spore sells a prefab setup that can work with fruiting blocks (which is what I tried) or spawn. The tub holds either substrate (for adding spawn or cultures) or fruiting blocks, while a humidifier, a humidistat, air filters, and an adjustable fan can take care of the environment.

It doesn’t take a whole lot of maintenance other than making sure the humidifier is full and the hose is attached and angled properly. I have successfully grown four rounds of pink, blue, and yellow oyster mushrooms, and three rounds of lion’s mane, all from fruiting blocks in the Boomr Bin. At times, I’ve had more mushrooms than I knew what to do with. If you’re looking to grow on a larger scale to sell or trade, North Spore also makes an even larger five-rack fruiting chamber called the BoomRoom, though I have not personally tested this.

Method 5: Bucket Tek

Difficulty 9/10 (Straw should be pasteurized) Aesthetics 7/10 (Potentially) Success 0/10 (Complete failure)

The “bucket tek” (or technique) is one of the most popular ways to grow mushrooms in large volumes. North Spore was born from its success in growing bucket mushrooms for restaurants near its home in Portland, Maine. Cofounder Matt McInnis recommends packing your bucket with chopped straw and grain spawn. The company sells a bucket tek starter kit—the MushBucket—which can produce high yields of oyster mushrooms. The kit comprises a package of chopped straw, a burlap bag, a strap, grain spawn, and a bucket with holes drilled into the sides through which the mushrooms can grow. (You can also buy refills of the straw and spawn for $40.)

The enclosed instructions give two options for preparing the straw, which you’ll stuff into the burlap bag: soak it in clean, cold water for a couple of hours and drip-dry overnight with the included strap, or boil the straw at a consistent 180 degrees Fahrenheit for one hour before proceeding with the drip-dry process. (I chose the former, as I found the burlap bag could only fit in a large cooler.) Afterward, alternate layers of straw with a thin layer of grain spawn until the bucket is full, then keep it in a humid place, and, finally, wait for it to colonize. I covered my bucket with an opaque garbage bag and kept it in an empty bathtub at room temperature for two weeks, but, sadly, the only colonization I saw was mold. If I did it again, I would pasteurize the straw and attempt the process outdoors, during the fall or spring.

Power up with unlimited access to WIRED. Get best-in-class reporting and exclusive subscriber content that's too important to ignore. Subscribe Today.

entries.title: An Ebike Company Was Sued for Misleading Info on Safety. It Points to a Big Problem
entries.summary: Several Chinese businesses associated with the ebike brand Aipas settled a lawsuit with Amazon and the safety certification company UL in July.
entries.content:
Amazon and UL, a company that tests and certifies that products meet safety standards, settled a lawsuit in July with several Chinese companies that manufacture ebikes under the Aipas brand name.

The US companies accused the Chinese firms in January of claiming in Amazon listings and on Aipas’ website that the ebikes had been certified by UL, giving recognizable, brand-name assurance to online shoppers worried about risks like ebike lithium-ion battery fires. But that wasn’t true. On July 15, a federal judge signed off on a permanent injunction in which the companies agreed that they would no longer use the UL mark. It also bars them from “assisting, aiding, or abetting any other person or business” in doing the same.

The filing notes that the Chinese companies didn’t admit liability or wrongdoing tied to Amazon’s and UL’s accusations of knowingly using fraudulent safety-certification marks. (The enterprises involved are Jiangmen Meijiasheng Bicycle, Hong Kong Manchester International Trading, Shenzhen Aibosi Sport Technology, Guangzhou Aierfeile Sport Technology, and the individual Tang Shuhui, who the lawsuit alleges was involved in an Amazon selling account.)

But WIRED has found a Facebook account associated with Aipas Ebike still running ads that include UL’s bright red logo. A WIRED reporter was served one of the ads in mid-July, even after the companies had agreed to a permanent injunction. At least one other ad using the UL mark has been actively running on Facebook since April, months after the lawsuit was filed, according to Meta's Ad Library. Another active ad including the UL mark and first posted in May appeared to disappear from the Ad Library after WIRED contacted both Aipas and UL for comment.

WIRED also found that while some Aipas ebikes and their batteries did eventually receive safety certifications from a nationally recognized organization, seemingly months after the company began advertising its UL safety marks, some of those certificates are now inactive.

A spokesperson for Amazon declined to comment on the lawsuit. Bradley Carlson, a spokesperson for UL, also declined to comment on the lawsuit but said the UL team is “aware and working to resolve” the issues related to the Facebook ads. Neither Aipas Ebike nor the lawyer representing the Chinese businesses in court responded to multiple requests for comment.

While fights over fraudulent product safety certifications don’t always break out into public view, “this is a common problem and has been a common problem for quite some time,” says Donald Mays, a longtime product safety expert who now runs his own consulting firm.

The trend has troubling implications, especially for electric mobility devices like ebikes. A report published by the US government in April found that 45 fatalities between 2017 and 2024 were associated with fires related to lithium-ion batteries in mobility devices, 19 of them related to ebikes alone. The New York City Fire Department, which tracks lithium-ion battery fires, logged 277 fires tied to the tech in 2024, compared with 268 in 2023, and linked them to ebikes.

Many of the country’s ebike fires were associated with replacement battery packs, as noted by the Consumer Product Safety Commission, an independent federal agency. The agency has urged consumers to use only micromobility devices “designed, manufactured, and certified for compliance by an accredited laboratory with the applicable consensus safety standards,” according to its website.

The false certification is more prevalent on online marketplaces like Amazon, says Gabe Knight, a senior policy analyst at Consumer Reports. “Just as consumers are more at risk to buy a counterfeit product, period, from third-party sellers, they are more at risk to buy a product with a fabricated certification mark,” she says. In general, retailers who sell products to consumers directly, rather than simply hosting sales, do a better job of guaranteeing their merchandise’s safety claims, she says; these retailers usually have internal compliance programs to verify manufacturers’ basic safety claims.

“It’s really polluting the marketplace when consumers can’t trust the information they see online,” Knight says.

The US government seems to be interested in cracking down on fake safety certifications. In May, the Consumer Product Safety Commission posted that it is “aware of instances in which the misuse of certification marks appears to be part of broader efforts to evade US safety requirements.” It asked for public input to better understand the prevalence of counterfeit marks.

Separately, in June, the CPSC proposed a rule to establish federal mandatory safety standards for the lithium-ion batteries used in micromobility products, including ebikes. (Some but not all states and local governments require lithium-ion batteries sold in their localities to meet mandatory safety standards, though there are federal standards for shipping batteries.) The agency will accept public comments through August. Spokespeople for the CPSC did not respond to WIRED’s questions.

Scott Hardesty, a YouTube ebike reviewer who posted videos critical of Aipas last fall, says his interactions with the company pushed him to create a new reviewing procedure. “After dealing with them, I started making companies send me their UL certificates,” he tells WIRED. “I need them to send them to me in an email.” (Hardesty says he receives commissions from companies for ebike sales made through his YouTube channel.)

In the Aipas lawsuit, lawyers for UL and Amazon said the companies were cracking down on counterfeits and fraudulent claims. They pointed to seven other recent legal actions against companies using fraudulent safety certification marks, including one 2023 case in which both Amazon and UL sued several Chinese companies for illegally using UL marks on smoke detectors without obtaining the required safety testing.

Last year, New York City nixed a plan to use Fly ebikes in a new delivery-worker bike-swap program after the company and UL agreed to a $1 million settlement in which Fly stipulated that it had used counterfeit UL marks. Streetsblog reported on eight lawsuits claiming the company was associated with fire-related injuries and deaths.

The Aipas lawsuit says that Amazon invested more than a billion dollars and employed thousands of people in 2024 to protect customers and brands from counterfeit and fraudulent goods. It says its system “automatically and continuously scans thousands of data points to prevent, detect, and remove counterfeits from its stores and to terminate the selling accounts of bad actors before they can offer counterfeit products.”

High Standards

Safety standards and the workings of the bodies that certify products can be confusing and difficult to understand. The nonprofit UL Standards and Engagement is one of the most reputable standards-writing organizations in the world, though others, including CSA Group and IEC, also write standards. But, for a fee, a once-related but now separate firm, UL Solutions, also tests products to guarantee they meet UL standards; only companies that test their products through UL are permitted to use UL’s logos in advertising.

Other organizations, called Nationally Recognized Testing Laboratories, have been authorized by the US government to test products to UL’s and other safety standards. Companies choose one lab over another mostly because of price, Mays says.

Documents posted on Aipas’ website suggest that some of its ebikes and batteries did receive safety certifications eventually from one of these authorized companies, in this case, SGS. But a closer look at the documents reveals irregularities.

The Amazon and UL lawsuit alleges that Aipas marketed its ebikes with the UL marks “at various times” from 2024 through 2025. In September 2025, according to an SGS certificate, an SGS employee certified that the lithium-ion batteries used in Aipas ebikes met a UL safety standard. At the end of that month, according to another SGS certificate, an SGS employee certified ebikes in the “M” Aipas line to the same standard. At the end of November, ebikes in the “A” and “F’ Aipas lines were also certified.

But while the company’s battery certificate is still active, the three certificates for the ebikes as a whole are not. In an email, a representative for SGS wrote that “as a third party, we are not at liberty to discuss the status of a certification.”

As of July, Aipas’ website accurately describes its lithium-ion batteries as “SGS Certified” to a UL safety standard. But it also continues to link to the inactive SGS ebike safety certificates, which buyers might discover only by following the QR code at the bottom of the certificate, or by looking up an individual product in SGS’ online directory.

An inactive safety certificate isn’t necessarily a sign that a product that was once certified is now unsafe, Mays says. Certificates can expire if a company changes a design or component on its product and doesn’t pay a certification body to recertify it. A product might also lose its certification if the company doesn’t submit its product to several inspections a year.

Still, when Mays bought his own ebikes, he made sure that they met the two related UL safety certifications and that their certificates were valid.

For people who aren’t product safety experts, that kind of document-forward legwork might be beyond reach. “The average consumer is not going to be able to figure this out,” Hardesty says.

Updated: 7/30/2026, 5:00 pm EDT: This story has been updated to clarify the relationship between UL Standards and Engagement and UL Solutions.

entries.title: Elite Young Runners Are Becoming Freakishly Fast. Welcome to ‘Trackflation’
entries.summary: Four-minute miles are almost routine. Middle schoolers are beating college runners. Inside the youth track boom that’s turning kids and teens into speed machines.
entries.content:
A couple of years ago, my teenage son decided to spend the long New York winter running indoor track. An accomplished soccer player, he joined the team with the modest goal of staying fit during his main sport’s offseason, but before long his competitive streak kicked in. He trained like a demon for two months straight, brutalizing his legs and lungs with high-intensity workouts. The suffering paid off: His time in the 1,600-meter run (just shy of a mile) dropped so much that he earned a spot in our sectional championship, competing against runners from three nearby counties.

My son had the race of his life at sectionals, shaving several seconds off his personal best. He also got absolutely smoked. The top three boys all finished in well under 4 minutes and 30 seconds. The next six broke 4:38. By the time my son collapsed across the finish line in second-to-last place, the winner was deep into his post-race stretching routine.

Had teenage runners at mid-tier meets always been that absurdly fast? Based on my limited knowledge of track, I suspected not, so I looked up the 1,600-meter results from the 2015 sectionals. Back then, only three boys broke 4:40, and my son would have finished in the middle of the pack. I was now curious whether kids beyond my provincial corner of New York were getting a lot faster too.

I followed a few track social media accounts, and soon the algorithms deluged me with highlights of young runners obliterating records on what seemed like a daily basis. A 12th-grade girl from Ohio ran an indoor 2-miler in an otherworldly 9:37.15, breaking a national high school record that had stood since 2013. A 10th-grade boy from Texas ran an outdoor 100-meter dash in 9.92 seconds, just a tenth of a second off the collegiate record. Crunch the data on any race in virtually any school-age meet and you’ll find that the top and median times have plunged since the late 2010s, often by several seconds.

My favorite videos were made by a former University of Pittsburgh hurdler named Shyheim Wright, a master at channeling the bewilderment of track fans. “Pack it up, pack it up right now!” he marveled over footage of a ninth-grade girl from Georgia running 100 meters in 11.01 seconds, a time that made her faster than all but the very best college sprinters. “I’m so glad I don’t run no more, I’m so glad. The times now is getting ridiculous.”

The track world, I discovered, has coined a name for this much-discussed phenomenon of kids getting ultra-fast: trackflation. In June 2025 at the New Balance Nationals, an annual championship that draws around 6,000 top youth athletes, more than 100 kids beat previous meet records. “There might now be a hundred kids that run under 4:10 in the full mile,” says Will Rodgers, who ran track at the University of Alabama in Huntsville in the early 2010s and now co-owns RunningLane, a coaching and events business. “Back when we were competing in high school, it might’ve been like 20 at max.”

I had to know why kids today are essentially breaking track. And that meant trying to understand the dramatic shift happening at the sport’s roots.

In early May, I drove down to Baltimore to attend the East Coast International Showcase, a two-day outdoor track meet sponsored by the shoe company Puma. One of its main draws was an 800-meter race featuring two fixtures of the elite youth circuit: Henry Bartle of Carmel, California, and Gunner Hammett of Williamsburg, Virginia.

As these two stars warmed up alongside Morgan State University’s faded blue track, I spotted Hammett’s dad wearing a custom hoodie that listed his son’s credentials: “2 Time World Record Holder 800m, 18 Time All-American, 5-Time National Record Holder.”

Those achievements were all the more impressive given Hammett’s tender age: He was 13, just a bit younger than the 14-year-old Bartle. Both boys were still weeks away from their eighth-grade graduations.

The thin-limbed, bleached-blond Bartle ran the two-lap race in a personal best of 2:03.52, an outrageously good time. Three years ago that would have been enough to win the middle-school 800 meters at the New Balance Nationals. But he was nowhere close to Hammett, who coasted to a 1:57.75—a performance that put him on par with his home state’s fastest high school runners.

“Guess I got smoked by you again,” a genial Bartle told Hammett as they gasped for air past the finish line. Bartle has, unfortunately, grown accustomed to falling short. Seven weeks earlier, he and another kid both shattered California’s age-group record for the indoor mile, but Bartle still finished six seconds off the podium. In the one-line caption for his Instagram post about that race, Bartle captured the essence of trackflation: “Living in a time where a state record still doesn’t medal at Nationals.”

Once Bartle had recouped enough oxygen to function, he gave me a thoughtful breakdown of his recent progress. “Last year, even if I had somewhat of an injury, I was very stubborn and I trained through it,” he told me. Now he’s prioritizing recovery as part of his regimen, and he believes a sub-two-minute 800 and a sub-4:30 mile are well within his grasp.

Until seven or eight years ago, few eighth-grade runners operated with Bartle’s degree of maturity and determination. There were no middle-school divisions at the big national meets, and 13- and 14-year-olds rarely put serious weekly mileage on their legs. But perhaps inevitably, the youth sports boom expanded to include track.

As millions of exhausted parents can attest, sports such as soccer and baseball have become dauntingly competitive over the past generation. Even for third and fourth graders, there’s a constant scramble for spots on prestigious travel teams, followed by nerve-racking fights for playing time. Some families decided they could avoid that madness by steering their kids toward track, which had relatively few participants at its entry-level ranks.

Grade schoolers began flocking to local track clubs. Dennis Robinson, head coach of the Zephyrs Track Club in Chicago, told me that for most of the 2010s he only managed to sign up high schoolers. “We were concerned about the future of our club,” he said. “Now we’re starting to see more and more younger athletes. And when I say young, I mean we’ve had to turn away athletes who are 4 years old.” Another club I spoke with, the Youth Track Foundation in Portland, Oregon, signed up 60 kids in its first two years of existence and has a lengthy waiting list.

These clubs have also benefited from track’s growing reputation as an antidote to the politics and favoritism that plague less objective sports. “I have so many parents that join our club, and they’re just like, ‘This is so great, because we had this terrible experience with the baseball team because this parent was coaching and he never let my kid pitch,’” says Brad Peterson, a cofounder of HP Distance, a track club in Indiana. “But in track there’s no argument.” The stopwatch determines everything.

The rise of track clubs was apparent on the second day of the East Coast International Showcase, when I watched wave after wave of kids aged 5 to 9 compete in the 100 meters. The many standouts ran times that would be dazzling for boys or girls several years older, and I took note of their efficient mechanics—evidence, it seemed to me, that they’re already being coached to develop good habits.

When talented elementary-age kids from the club system move into their tweens and early teens, they’re primed to start the grueling training that used to be reserved for high schoolers. “I’m asking middle-school girls to do eight 400s in 75 seconds, so at a 5-minute-mile pace,” says Peterson. “Whereas it used to be the middle-school program was, ‘Hey, let’s jog 20 minutes and let’s go play Capture the Flag,’ right?”

Kids are increasingly doing that training in so-called “super shoes” such as the Nike Vaporfly 4 or New Balance FuelCell SuperComp Elite v5, both of which now often sell for well under $200. These shoes’ carbon plates and ultralight foams help return kinetic energy to the wearer’s body with every step. In races, super shoes have been proven to boost performance by 2 to 3 percent, which translates to roughly a second per 400-meter lap.

Geoff Burns, a sports physiologist with the US Olympic & Paralympic Committee, believes the shoes’ energy-return component decreases the traumatic stress endured by young bones and muscles. “If you’re able to do that same work but not have that mechanical collateral damage or nearly as much of it, then you’re just not building up as much scar tissue that could potentially inhibit performance,” he says. Instead of diverting lots of cellular energy to the healing process after every workout, the body can dedicate more resources to building strength.

As middle schoolers have gotten faster, big-name meets have made room for them. New Balance Nationals added its middle-school division in 2023, and other prominent track events followed suit. Athletes like Bartle now spend a significant chunk of every winter and spring traveling across the country.

The results make track old-timers shake their heads with wonder. In April, for example, an eighth-grade boy shattered the national middle-school record for the mile (he ran it sub-4:18). Just two weeks later, an eighth-grade girl ran the 400 meters faster than any US high schooler had at that point in the year (she clocked 51.58).

It’s not just the superstars who are getting faster. The runners in the pack behind them are picking up the pace, too, and they’re transforming what’s considered a decent time. “I think I have 10 middle-school girls who run between a 4:56 and a 5:16 mile,” says Peterson. “Ten years ago, you’d be like, ‘That’s insane.’ Now it’s nothing.” When these kids head to high school, they have the strength and experience to flourish right away. But given the sky-high expectations they face, the intensity can come at a cost.

There has long been a vast discrepancy between the number of high schoolers who run track and the money their programs receive. Outdoor track and field boasts more high school athletes than any other sport, with over 1.1 million participants—a figure likely inflated by the fact that teams tend to make few if any cuts. (The second-most populous co-ed sport, basketball, has fewer than 900,000 players.) Many athletic departments treat track like an afterthought, undeserving of the resources needed to hire coaches with real expertise.

“When I was coming through, my coach was a retired Marine who had never run a step in his life,” says Caryn Gehrke, an all–Ivy League runner at Columbia University in the early 2000s who now coaches in Ann Arbor, Michigan. “His entire philosophy was the more you hurt in practice, the more you can hurt in a race,” which is possibly the most counterproductive advice a runner can hear.

Today, a high school runner can scrutinize workout videos posted by pros or simply ask ChatGPT “How do I run a sub-2:00 800?” and develop a more effective training plan than anything their forebears were using in 2016. But for runners with any financial means, hiring a private coach for one-on-one guidance has become commonplace. It’s easy to find former collegiate runners on Instagram who are eager to capitalize on their years of geeking out on training methods. These coaches have popularized techniques such as heat acclimatization, in which athletes overexert themselves in sweltering conditions—say, by running on a treadmill next to a space heater—so that their bodies will produce extra plasma. (This is sometimes referred to as “legal blood doping.”)

They’re also a big reason why high school kids are dabbling in double-threshold training, which was pioneered by Norwegian pros. This approach involves doing a hard morning workout, then analyzing a drop of the athlete’s blood to see how much lactic acid their muscles produced. The runner then adjusts their pace up or down during a killer second workout of the day.

Private coaches also preach about proper nutrition and supplements. Survey the trash cans at any meet and you’ll see empty tubs of Maurten Bicarb System, a sodium bicarbonate powder that runners consume en masse before races. The substance suppresses the development of lactic acid in muscles, allowing runners to stave off fatigue for a tad longer than normal. According to a 2021 meta survey, bicarb may boost performance by 1.5 percent, or around three seconds per mile.

There’s plenty of chatter that some teen athletes are emulating the pros by ingesting banned substances—a cheat that’s almost impossible to detect, since there’s virtually no drug testing at the high school level. “I’m not saying I’m 100 percent on this, but I can pick out the athletes who are on it,” says Timothy Goldsack, a cofounder of the coaching service RunCCG, who cites human growth hormone and testosterone-replacement therapy as two popular performance enhancers. He blames familial pressure on kids to use track as a ticket to college: “Parents are crazy, you know that.”

To impress college scouts, high schoolers must travel to the national meets that are known for producing the most trackflationary times. These meets tend to have bouncy polyurethane tracks, favorable weather, or pace lights that keep runners apprised of the tempos needed to break records. A few well-known teen runners receive airfare and hotel rooms for gracing these meets with their presence, but most competitors spend small fortunes to attend. “If a family has the money to invest in that and it costs them $2,000 to fly a kid across the country to take 15 seconds off their time, and that all of a sudden opens the door to another tier of scholarship or whatever, that’s a pretty good investment,” says Geoff Burns of the US Olympic & Paralympic Committee.

In mid-April, I spoke to Chris Catton, the other cofounder of RunCCG, as he prepared to coach several of his clients through a notoriously fast meet, the Asics Carolina Distance Carnival in North Carolina. “I have people who have flown here from Montana, North Dakota, Oklahoma, Rhode Island, New Jersey,” he told me by phone from his Airbnb in Charlotte. He was excited because the racing would take place at night, when times are fastest, and runners would be grouped together in heats according to their personal bests—a reliable way to get kids to push each other in a tight pack.

Two days later, Catton texted me a heads-up to check out the Carnival’s results. They were staggering: In the high school boys’ mile, the top 14 finishers all went sub-4:10. Five middle-school girls had broken the 5-minute barrier. Catton said that one of his clients, a ninth-grade girl who ran the 3,200 meters, had set an Oklahoma state record at the Carnival, yet only managed to come in 14th—half a minute behind the winner.

The social media content generated by these meets has turned a number of high schoolers into influencers. Some of them get sponsorship deals. Most receive perks rather than money—notably loads of free swag that they’re encouraged to flaunt online—though that may change as brands increasingly realize how much high school stars can shape the tastes of young consumers.

I watched one of those sought-after celebrities, 17-year-old Natalie Dumas, compete at the East Coast International Showcase. The New Jersey native had been one of the most recognizable faces of trackflation for nearly a year, ever since she’d won an incredible three races at the New Balance Nationals in June 2025. In Baltimore, she warmed up for a mere 20 minutes before demolishing the field in the 400-meter hurdles. She looked almost bored as she finished in 58.78; it wasn’t until I checked her Instagram later that I realized it was (at least at that moment) the fastest a high school girl had run the race this year.

When I caught up with Dumas right after the race, she implied that she would have run much faster if she wasn’t in such high demand. “I ran Wednesday, Thursday, Friday, and today’s Saturday, so this is my fourth meet this week,” she told me. “This is my sixth event in the past four days.”

For a brief moment in April, a stunning video was everywhere in my Instagram feed. It showed a 12-year-old girl from Utah running the mile in 4:40.98, a time that crushed the previous world record for her age group by nearly six seconds. She might have run even faster had she not elected to celebrate with the Scuba Dance a few steps short of the finish line.

The feat made Shyheim Wright, the content creator who specializes in highlighting the most ludicrous examples of trackflation, lose his mind. “Nah, bro, nah, bro, no way!” he began his Instagram post. “Bro, nobody was even in the camera with her, bro. There was other people in the race. I didn’t see nobody.” (“She was in my heat,” wrote one commenter, a seventh grader from North Carolina who ran her personal best in the race. “I didn’t see her once, she was so far ahead.”)

To many longtime track observers, however, the video was a cause for alarm. Multiple coaches and researchers told me this particular world record crystallized their fears for the safety of young runners who feel compelled to keep topping their peers. “When you see these amazing performances, it’s also just the tip of the spear of a lot of people also falling apart,” says Douglas Casa, a kinesiology professor at the University of Connecticut who studies elite athletes. “I’m not excited about all this progress. It just means that they’re working so much harder at a younger age.” He’s especially worried about young girls who specialize in middle- to long-distance events, since running the mileage required to excel in these races can delay puberty, leading to long-term issues such as lower bone density.

Burnout has always been a major problem in track, a sport anchored in athletes’ ability to sop up unnatural amounts of punishment. “I’ve spent my whole life running a race fueled by fear, never-ending expectations, and external validation,” wrote Stanford University 800-meter specialist Roisin Willis in a 2025 Instagram post about overcoming her mental health struggles; around the same time, Chloe Foerster, an All American middle-distance runner at the University of Washington, went public about her serious anxiety, which often caused her to vomit before races.

One 2022 study looked at the careers of 67,600 top-ranked teenage track-and-field athletes. Less than a quarter of 16- and 17-year-olds achieved comparable success past those ages, a transition rate the study authors described as “low.” Burns told me the results are even worse for kids who dominate at 16 or younger. “It’s a Faustian bargain,” he says. “Like, do you hyper-specialize and develop as a middle schooler and high schooler so you can get access and get scholarships and open all these doors, but it comes at the cost of actually being able to be good at it later?”

I didn’t meet a single young runner who showed any trace of having pondered that question—naivete is one of the blessings of youth. They were instead wrapped up in envying the kids whose increasingly impressive victories get pumped into their phones every day: “When you see a girl run a faster time, all of them are trying to attack it, and they all have the mindset that they want to win,” said Dumas, the 17-year-old phenom.

Whenever it seems like times are about to plateau in a certain race, another 14- or 15-year-old emerges to set a new standard and stoke the competition. (That Utah girl who set the internet alight with a 4:40 mile in April? She beat her own record by two seconds in June.) Jesse Williams, a meet organizer in Seattle, used to give coaches a rough estimate of what times would earn their athletes a spot at the Brooks PR, an elite invitational that he helps oversee. He had to stop—records are falling too fast for Williams to keep up.

All that progress, of course, is built atop a foundation of pain, and I had to ask the kids why they love a sport that primarily tests their ability to endure agony. The answer that stuck with me most came from Dane Szczepkowski, an 18-year-old sprinter from SPIRE Academy in Geneva, Ohio. I caught him during a rough moment in Baltimore, mere minutes after he’d finished 39th in the 400 meters; he was clearly struggling as he tried to stretch away the damage he’d done to his legs. When I asked him why he’d become so devoted to running, he thought for a beat before offering an emphatic answer. “It’s hard,” he said. “It’s hard.”

I’d heard that sentiment from a lot of runners. They had chosen track specifically because it’s an unglamorous grind—a lonely contest between them and a pitiless clock. The joy is in learning to appreciate the cause-and-effect relationship between arduous labor and incremental progress, even when that progress delivers a middle-of-the-pack result. Sophia Belcher, a high school junior from the Dallas area who won a Texas state title in the 800 meters this May, told me how the never-ending demands of track have been her rock through the most difficult moments of adolescence: “In a world where I think a lot of things are changing and not constant, I kind of had running that I could focus on, that would always be there.”

There’s obviously nothing new about unmoored teens searching for structure, but track’s rise does feel connected to the unique tumult of the past few years. Kids are understandably reeling from the echoes of the pandemic, social media, AI. Track just might be the ideal sport for those who tend to turn inward when looking to build self-belief. Amid the acceleration of everything, there’s clarity to be found in trying to get a tenth of a second faster.

My son was good-natured about his thumping loss at the 2025 sectionals, but that race turned out to be the apex of his running career. He ran winter track again as a senior but his heart wasn’t in it—he grew averse to the harsh training for the 1,600 and dropped down to shorter races, where faster sprinters dominated. A second trip to sectionals wasn’t in the cards, which didn’t bother him; by season’s end, he’d become more interested in trying to learn how to kick field goals so he can walk on to his college’s football team in the fall.

But our family’s journey through track seems far from over. My 13-year-old daughter joined her middle-school team this spring, and after some trial and error, she settled on the 800 as her primary race. She gutted out some decent times at a few meets, and her coach rewarded her with a spot in a post-season meet held in a nearby college town.

She was nervous for days beforehand, and I tried to assure her that just showing up and running would be a victory—no one expects her to be the next Natalie Dumas. On the afternoon of the meet, though, she called me to report that things had gone much better than expected. Not only had she conquered her butterflies, she’d also won her heat. She’d looked over her shoulder with about 50 meters to go and realized that no one was even close to overtaking her. “I pulverized those other girls,” she said with evident glee.

She’s still light-years away from a shout-out in a Shyheim Wright video. But in the era of trackflation, all things seem possible.

The images for this story were photographed at the New Balance Nationals Outdoor on June 21, 2026, at Franklin Field in Philadelphia.

Let us know what you think about this article. Submit a letter to the editor at [email protected].

entries.title: The ACLU Is Arming Lawyers to Expose State Surveillance Secrets
entries.summary: A new toolkit for attorneys in Massachusetts targets the technologies police use—and conceal—to build criminal cases, from facial recognition to AI-written police reports.
entries.content:
The American Civil Liberties Union of Massachusetts says it’s releasing an online toolkit this week for criminal defense attorneys designed to uncover whether police used surveillance technologies—facial recognition, automatic license plate readers, gunshot detection systems, and more—to build the cases against their clients in secret.

The toolkit, which the group describes as the first of its kind, is built around handcrafted legal motions that, when granted by a judge, will force prosecutors to reveal whether surveillance technology was used against a defendant. It covers an array of technologies, from “stingray” phone trackers and location data sold by commercial brokers to AI-drafted police reports and forensic tools that crack phones and siphon data from car infotainment systems.

It also includes preservation motions—demands that surveillance data be saved before it is deleted automatically—aimed at government agencies and private vendors alike. The filings rest on a bedrock rule of American criminal law, established in 1963, that prosecutors are required to turn over all evidence that could help the defense. The motions will be distributed through a password-protected library, the ACLU of Massachusetts says, available only to verified defense attorneys.

The group is fighting a war against unregulated surveillance tools on two fronts, says Jennifer Herrmann, a staff attorney at the ACLU of Massachusetts and one of the toolkit’s authors. One front is pressuring cities to drop tools like Flock, the license plate reader network, and ShotSpotter, the gunshot detection system, outright. The other is arming defense lawyers to expose the surveillance in court.

“The amount of time that it can take for that initial use of technology and investigation to actually get to a court and constitutional challenge is long,” Herrmann says—so the motions give attorneys a way to “challenge what has already happened in the past,” building a record that carries over to whatever tool comes next.

The project responds to what its authors describe as a chronic lag between the moment police adopt a new surveillance tool and the moment courts finally decide whether using it is legal. Most Americans carried cell phones by the late 1990s, but the United States Supreme Court did not require police to get a warrant for cell phone location records until 2018. The lag isn’t just the result of slow-moving courts. The technologies with the worst disclosure records have faced the fewest court rulings precisely because a defendant cannot challenge a tool they were never told about.

In materials accompanying the launch, the ACLU of Massachusetts says police deploy these tools “sometimes without even disclosing their use to the prosecutors handling the case.” For years, the FBI required local police departments to sign secrecy agreements before using cell-site simulators—suitcase-sized devices, commonly called stingrays, that impersonate cell towers to secretly locate phones. In Baltimore, one such agreement instructed prosecutors to drop criminal cases entirely rather than reveal the device in court, even as the city’s police used it more than 4,300 times between 2007 and 2015. FBI records later obtained by the ACLU show the agreements persisted until at least 2020.

When Missouri investigators used Fog Reveal, a commercial tool that maps people’s movements using app-harvested location data, in the investigation of a 2017 murder, the Associated Press found no trace of it in the trial exhibits that helped convict the victim’s wife. And when Louisiana authorities obtained an arrest warrant in 2022 for Randal Quran Reid—a Georgia man misidentified by facial recognition and jailed for nearly a week—the warrant attributed his identification not to software but to “a credible source,” according to The Washington Post. The very document that authorized his arrest actively obscured how police came to suspect him.

The toolkit’s authors frame the problem slightly differently: Their central worry is not only evidence hidden from prosecutors but defendants who are never told a surveillance tool touched their case at all, and defense lawyers who don't know to ask.

Gideon Epstein, a Technology for Liberty policy counsel at the civil liberties group and the toolkit’s other author, points to Massachusetts's own leading license plate reader case, Commonwealth v. McCarthy, in which the defense sought records for only a handful of cameras rather than the statewide network. “A lot of the time they're not asking for the full scope of surveillance technologies in use,” Epstein says. The motions, he adds, are as much about prompting lawyers to demand everything as about forcing the government to cough it up.

The preservation motions target a separate and subtler problem: surveillance evidence that destroys itself. Axon's Draft One, an AI tool that writes police reports from body camera audio, discards the machine-written first draft by design. An Axon product manager told the Electronic Frontier Foundation in July 2025 that the choice avoids creating “disclosure headaches” for police customers and prosecutors. California legislators saw the matter differently and enacted a law last year that requires agencies to keep the original drafts.

Elsewhere, Flock Safety’s license plate reader network deletes its footage after 30 days by default, and Chicago’s contract for the ShotSpotter gunshot detection system declared the gunfire data the vendor's own property, not the city’s. In each case, the digital material a defendant would need to fight back against a claim can vanish—or sit beyond the government's reach—before a defense attorney is ever appointed to the case.

Flock Safety spokesperson Paris Lewbel says the company has procedures to preserve the integrity and authenticity of data stored in its system, and tools to confirm that an ALPR image or investigative report has not been altered since it was pulled from the platform.

Lewbel says Flock complies with legal demands for customer data. Requests that don't legally bind Flock it refers to the customer, whom it also notifies.

A spokesperson for SoundThinking, maker of ShotSpotter, says the company complies with subpoenas and court orders regardless of which side issues them, and cooperates with “reasonable requests” for data in criminal proceedings. When a subpoena covers material the company considers confidential, such as its own trade secrets, it asks courts to restrict that material's dissemination.

Epstein is careful to note the ACLU still generally wants this data to quickly disappear. It’s lobbying Massachusetts to cut license plate reader retention to as little as two days, from Flock's default of 30. But once someone is facing charges, he says, that same data can be the evidence that clears them, which is what the preservation motions are designed to protect.

If the approach sounds theoretical, Massachusetts's highest court has recently proven otherwise, twice. In 2024, the state’s Supreme Judicial Court permanently threw out drug and firearm charges against a Boston man—barring prosecutors from ever refiling them—after police defied a court order to turn over records of fake social media profiles that officers used to surveil him, built with predominantly Black and brown avatars. Last year, the same court ruled that defendants can demand a hearing forcing prosecutors to prove ShotSpotter actually works before its evidence reaches a jury. The rulings help explain why the toolkit is built on Massachusetts law, which the ACLU says can be adapted for other states: The state’s high court has run years ahead of the US Supreme Court on digital privacy, requiring warrants for cell phone location data back in 2014.

The ACLU’s timing is not incidental: Three weeks ago, the US Supreme Court ruled 6-3 that when police use a so-called geofence warrant—an order forcing Google to identify virtually all phones near a crime scene—they are conducting a “search” under the Fourth Amendment, triggering the Constitution's privacy protections. The justices sent the case back down for lower courts to work out what a valid geofence warrant looks like. The ACLU’s toolkit will ship a geofencing motion directly into those unsettled waters.

The motions are meant to be adapted and filed in real criminal cases, and the group is asking attorneys who use them to report back. The point, Herrmann says, is to take good lawyering that already exists and put it “at the fingertips of every attorney for every defendant,” while continuing to flag the gaps where new strategies are needed.

Updated at 1:10 pm ET, July 20, 2026: Added comments from Flock Safety and SoundThinking.

entries.title: Apps Marketed to US Troops Are Shipping Chinese and Russian Code
entries.summary: A first-of-its-kind analysis found more than one in eight apps built for US service members carried foreign code—some from firms in nations the Pentagon designates as adversaries.
entries.content:
A recent examination of hundreds of mobile apps marketed toward US military personnel found more than one in eight contained software built by companies in China, Russia, or other foreign nations, raising fresh concerns that adversary governments could harvest data revealing where service members live, work, and deploy.

According to researchers at Purdue University, the US Military Academy at West Point, and Florida International University, one popular app used by service members to rate living conditions on their own bases include code from Huawei, the Chinese telecom that US regulators flagged as a national security threat in 2020. Two others were built by Russian companies and incorporate the Russian ad service Yandex.

The largely unregulated advertising industry that tracks Americans online treats civilians and service members mostly the same—unless there is profit in telling them apart—despite evidence that exposure can reveal troop deployments, unit movements, and the routines of personnel within intelligence facilities and hardened shelters where nuclear weapons are believed to be stored.

WIRED investigations have previously shown location data harvested from ordinary apps tracing US service members to their homes, their children's schools, and off-base establishments where troops are prohibited from being seen. Experts have warned the same data could aid foreign spies in identifying personnel with access to sensitive sites, map when a facility is least guarded, or surface other compromising details.

The stakes are no longer hypothetical. In April, US Central Command acknowledged in a letter to Senator Ron Wyden that it had received multiple threat reports of adversaries exploiting commercial location data to target or surveil American personnel in the Middle East, where US forces remain locked in a standoff with the Iranian military over the Strait of Hormuz. Lawmakers called it the first official confirmation that troops in an active war zone were being hunted through the data-broker economy—a threat the Pentagon's own contractors and researchers had warned about for nearly a decade.

The new study takes a first look at one piece of that exposure: what actually sits inside the apps built and marketed specifically for the military.

"We are grateful for the opportunity to bring greater attention to these issues,” says Joshua Shinkle, a Purdue University PhD researcher and the study’s lead author. “We hope the research helps military-affiliated personnel, developers, and platforms make more informed privacy decisions and encourages continued discussion with developers, platforms, and policymakers about how to address these gaps.”

The researchers examined more than 220 such apps—from uniform guides and promotion-exam prep to banking and dating apps—pulled from the Google Play store and military subreddits. Nearly two-thirds—or 64 percent—contained third-party code, known as SDKs: prebuilt software components, typically used for analytics and advertising, that can also track user behavior, including their locations, and share that information with outside companies.

Forty percent of the apps collected or shared more data than they disclosed in their Google or Apple store listings, the researchers found.

The most common SDKs came from Google and Facebook, the two companies that dominate US digital advertising. But 76 turned up in all, including code traced back to China, Russia, Israel, India, Germany, and others. Roughly 7 percent of the apps carried third-party code from a nation considered adversarial by the Pentagon.

Twelve of the apps contained HMS Core, a Huawei software kit that advertises the ability to map user locations, deliver ads, and store images and video. Several were built for state National Guard organizations.

The researchers observed no data actually going to Huawei servers. But an SDK can be updated remotely at any time. Code that is dormant today can still be spyware tomorrow. In at least one case, noted by the study, the Huawei code arrived without the app’s developer’s knowledge, smuggled in as a dependency in a commercial notification tool.

The researchers also surveyed 103 military-affiliated Americans—active-duty service members, reservists, veterans, DoD civilians, and their families—about the data practices they encountered on their own phones. More than 83 percent used at least one app engaging in data practices they said made them uncomfortable. On average, those participants used more than three such apps.

Between 76 and 83 percent of participants said they were extremely uncomfortable with apps containing code from China, Russia, Iran, or North Korea—the four nations the Pentagon designates as cyber adversaries. But no user has a straightforward way to know which apps carry such code. Neither Google's Play Store Data Safety section nor Apple's App Store Privacy Labels disclose the country of origin of the software running inside an app.

Participants reported being more comfortable with data collection when an app was branded for military use.

Meanwhile, nearly two-thirds said they had received little or no institutional guidance on personal app use. Of those who had received some, nearly three-quarters called it inadequate.

The Pentagon declined to comment.

Asked to weigh potential mitigations, participants ranked in-phone warnings—alerts when foreign or unknown third-party code is present in an installed app—as both the most effective and the most likely for them to support.

A federal law restricting data brokers from buying or selling data on military-affiliated personnel, independent audits of app privacy disclosures, and stricter bans on foreign code in military-marketed apps drew nearly identical support.
